summary: I started working for the Works Progress Administration in March of 1938 after being out of work for several months. My first job was in the sewing room, but I was soon transferred to the Housekeeping Aid Project, where I was made supervisor in October of last year. The WPA work was a blessing during those hard times, providing jobs for people like me and helping others who couldn't help themselves. We trained women to assist families with practical nursing and housekeeping, which was very rewarding.

I was raised right here in Clarke County, Georgia, as the oldest of seven children. Growing up, we lived near Barnett Shoals and my father was an overseer on a large farm. Clarke County is where I went to school and Sunday school in a country schoolhouse, and where I joined the Oolinth Baptist Church at age 14. It’s been my lifelong home and the place where many of my memories and experiences took place.

We lived near Barnett Shoals, and I had a cousin who worked in the cotton mill there. I remember selling buttermilk to the men building the electric plant at Barnett Shoals when I was young. It was a place where surplus farm supplies could be sold to mill workers, and it was part of my early life and work experience. Later, my husband and I were transferred from Barnett Shoals to Tallassee, Alabama, in 1926.

In 1926, my husband and I were transferred to Tallassee, Alabama, where he worked at the power plant. Tragically, he was killed at the plant there when I was putting the children to bed after a vacation. His death was caused by a broken insulator and damp clothing, and it was a devastating event for our family. After his death, I stayed in the house provided by the company in Tallassee and worked to support my children.

When I was 14 years old, I bought a sewing machine by ordering it from Sears, Roebuck and Company. I paid for it by sewing for Negro families, which was how I earned money as a young girl. That sewing machine was important to me and marked a step toward independence and work outside the home.

I joined the Oolinth Baptist Church when I was 14 years old, and all my family are Baptists. We attended Sunday school in the same country schoolhouse where I went to school. I was baptized in Big Creek, which was near the church, and my faith has been an important part of my life.

Big Creek is where I was baptized when I joined the Oolinth Baptist Church at age 14. It’s not far from the church and the schoolhouse where I attended Sunday school and regular school. That creek holds a special place in my memories of growing up and my religious life.

My husband and I got married in Athens, Georgia, at my cousin’s house where her husband was a preacher. Athens was also where my husband worked for the Athens Railway and Electric Company, which later became Georgia Power. We lived there before being transferred to Tallassee, Alabama, and it was an important place in our early married life.

My husband worked for the Athens Railway and Electric Company, which later became Georgia Power. He started working there when he was 15 years old and eventually became plant manager, earning $165 a month. He was operating the power plant during the war and did not go to war himself. His work with Georgia Power was his lifelong career until his death at the Tallassee plant.

The Red Cross in our town sometimes sends a representative to our weekly group meetings for the Housekeeping Aid Project. They teach us how to make bandages, make beds, and care for the sick, which helps us do our work better. Their involvement is part of the training that improves the quality of care we provide to families in need.

A man who works at the University of Georgia came to me looking for a nurse to help his sick wife and son. He works at the barn on campus and was referred to me by the Welfare Office. Although our Aids are not nurses, I tried to help him as best I could, showing how our project assists families in need around here.

Sheriff Jackson was mentioned by a deputy sheriff named Carson who came to me asking for help. Carson said if I wanted a recommendation about him, I could call Sheriff Jackson to learn more. Sheriff Jackson’s name came up as a reference to vouch for the character of people seeking assistance from our project.
